Quote_Text: I adore the fullness and perfection of the Scripture.
Notes: Proves Scripture is perfect rule.

Quote_Text: Those doctrines which are contained in the Scriptures are sufficient for faith.
Notes: Patristic witness to Scripture's sufficiency.

Quote_Text: It is a manifest offense of arrogance and infidelity, either to reject any part of that which is written, or to add any other thing which is not written.
Notes: Proves Scripture is complete.

Quote_Text: Whether it concerns Christ, or His church, or any other thing belonging to our faith, or life, I will not say if we do preach, that even as the apostle himself says afterwards, if an angel comes from heaven with any other doctrine than that you have heard out of the legal and evangelical Scriptures, let him be accursed.
Notes: Scripture alone is the rule.

Quote_Text: Whereas our Savior Christ did many things, and yet all of them are not written: those things were chosen to be written which were sufficient for the salvation of believers.
Notes: Sufficiency of written Scripture.

Quote_Text: The Canon of Holy Scripture suffices for all things even abundantly.
Notes: Key patristic sola scriptura text.

Quote_Text: The books of the gospel contain doctrine replete with all manner of perfection.
Notes: Gospel sufficiency.

Quote_Text: It is evident that the Scripture sufficiently contains all things necessary for the pilgrimage to heaven.
Notes: Scholastic witness to Scripture sufficiency.

Quote_Text: We must have recourse only to the Scriptures, that we may obtain salvation.
Notes: Medieval conciliarist agrees with Reformed position.

Quote_Text: I say that all those things were written by the apostles which are necessary for all men, and which they publicly preached to all men.
Notes: Bellarmine's own concession used against him.

Quote_Text: In many other things I do embrace and love Nepos, but surely the truth is to be preferred and respected before all. If any man speaks well, we ought to give him his due praise and approbation without all envy, but if he speaks anything that is not sound, we must both examine it and try it thoroughly.
Notes: Fathers must be tested by truth.

Quote_Text: I will not bind you to my authority, that you should therefore think anything necessary to be believed because I affirm it.
Notes: Augustine disclaims binding authority.

Quote_Text: I have learned to give this honor and reverence to those books alone which are now called canonical, that I do infallibly believe that no author of them all, or any of them, ever erred in any of his writings. For others, I so read them that (however holy or learned they may be) I do not think this or that to be true because they so held it, but so far as they are able to persuade me, either by those canonical authors, or by probable reason, that it is not opposing the truth.
Notes: Classic Augustine statement on canonical vs. patristic authority.

Quote_Text: I am not tied to the authority of this epistle, because I do not hold Cyprian's letters for canonical, but I examine them by canonical Scriptures: and whatever I find therein consenting to them, I approve with his praise; and whatever is opposite to them, by his leave I must reject.
Notes: Augustine tests even Cyprian by Scripture.

Quote_Text: In those parts of Scripture which are most plain, we found all those things that contain the rules of our faith and manner, namely, hope and charity.
Notes: Scripture's perspicuity.

Quote_Text: If a man affirms anything without authority of Scripture, his auditors do but slenderly respect it.
Notes: Chrysostom on Scripture's authority.

Quote_Text: It is not sufficient for us to believe the fathers and writers of the Church in matters of faith, unless they confirm their sayings by Scripture.
Notes: Basil: Fathers must be confirmed by Scripture.

Quote_Text: We preach and observe some things out of the written word, and other some which we have received by mystical tradition from the apostles. And both these have like force and efficacy unto godliness.
Notes: Perkins uses this to show Basil contradicts himself vs. his Regulae.

Quote_Text: It is a tradition, inquire no further.
Notes: Shown as self-contradicting his own sola scriptura statements.

Quote_Text: If we preach anything without Scripture, our auditory will be most uncertain what to think, now assenting, now doubting, and sometimes plainly rejecting as frivolous the words they hear.
Notes: Chrysostom against himself on traditions.

Source_Work: Sermo de Sancto et Adorando Spiritu
Quote_Text: If you hear any say, I have the Holy Spirit, and yet he speaks not out of the gospel, but of his own fantasy, he speaks of himself, and the Holy Ghost is not in him. If any of those that are said to have the Holy Ghost, do speak ought besides the gospel of themselves, believe them not, but follow my doctrine.
Notes: Chrysostom's strong sola scriptura statement.

Quote_Text: Therefore is Christ called the Mediator of God and man, between immortal God and mortal man; God and man reconciling man unto God, remaining that which He was, and made that which He was not.
Notes: Christ as God-man Mediator.

Quote_Text: Christ's death is confessed to be profitable unto us, but if He were not God, how could He, and He alone suffice for the price of all our redemption? But He only in His death satisfied for all, because He is above all.
Notes: Christ's deity necessary for sufficient redemption.

Quote_Text: Neither could we be freed by that one Mediator of God and man, the man Christ, unless He were God.
Notes: Christ's deity essential to mediation.

Quote_Text: Besides these, there are other books of the Old Testament which are not canonical, but only read by the catechumens, as the book of Wisdom, Ecclesiasticus, etc.
Notes: Athanasius limits OT canon, excluding Apocrypha.

Quote_Text: Of these, read the 22 books, but meddle not with the Apocrypha. Meditate diligently upon those Scriptures which the Church confidently reads and use no other. The apostles and bishops, at the first sowing of the Word, who left us these books, were far wiser, far holier than you are; therefore since you are a son of the church, do not break the bounds they have set you.
Notes: Cyril strictly limits canon to 22 OT books.

Quote_Text: They are fit and profitable, but not reckoned among those which are approved by the church, and therefore they were neither laid up in Aaron, nor in the ark of the testament.
Notes: Epiphanius on Wisdom and Ecclesiasticus — useful but not canonical.

Quote_Text: He sits at God's right hand, but He has sent His deputy, namely, the power of the Holy Spirit to work upon the hearts of the faithful.
Notes: Holy Spirit speaks through Scripture.

Quote_Text: Since earth affords no judgment of this matter, we must seek a judge in heaven. But what need we knock at heaven, since we have here a testament in the gospel? An earthly father, feeling himself near death, and fearing the contention of his sons after his death, calling some witnesses, he transposes his will out of his dying breast into lasting parchment. If so be now contention do arise among the brethren, do they run to their father's grave? No, but seek out his will and testament, and he who sleeps in his tomb speaks lively out of the silent parchment.
Notes: Scripture as the testament / will of Christ settling controversies.

Quote_Text: This controversy requires a judge. Then let Christ be the judge, let Himself speak who it was that His death did profit, and with Him let the apostle also judge, because in the apostle Christ speaks.
Notes: Scripture as judge of controversy.

Quote_Text: If it be taught in the gospel, or contained in the epistles or Acts of the Apostles, that converts from heresies should not be baptized, but only have imposition of hands in sign of repentance, let this holy and divine tradition be observed.
Notes: Tradition defined as what is in Scripture.

Quote_Text: Custom without truth, is but an antiquity in error.
Notes: Classic Reformed proof-text against mere tradition.

Quote_Text: Every province thinks their fathers' precepts to be apostolic doctrines.
Notes: Shows tradition is relative to region, not universally apostolic.

Quote_Text: Do you not think that it is lawful for any Christian to invent and institute what agrees with God, assists discipline, and helps unto salvation?
Notes: Tertullian on custom — Perkins shows this proves too much.

Quote_Text: We must know that there passed many ages after the revelation of the gospel before these hours were ordained to be the solemnities of certain hours in the day and night as they are now.
Notes: Hours are medieval, not apostolic.

Quote_Text: [Epiphanius gives a venerable respect unto Mary, but denies her both adoration and invocation.]
Notes: Epiphanius denies adoration and invocation of Mary.

Quote_Text: He thinks them fools that do adore and worship their fellow servants.
Notes: Ambrose condemns worship of fellow servants.

Quote_Text: As it is meet and fit we should worship nothing but God, so ought we not to fasten our hopes upon any but upon God.
Notes: Basil: hope and worship for God alone.

Quote_Text: We do not worship nor adore, I do not say, the relics of the martyrs, but neither the sun nor the moon, no, nor the angels, lest we should be thought to serve the creature rather than the Creator.
Notes: Jerome denies adoration of relics.

Quote_Text: We neither affirm that the martyrs are gods, neither have we accustomed ourselves to adore them.
Notes: Cyril denies martyrs are gods or to be adored.

Quote_Text: You need not any patron before God, nor with long discourse to curry favor with others, but though you do come alone without ever a patron, and offer your prayers unto God by yourself, yet shall you obtain your petition. For God does not consent unto our request so easily when others pray for us, as when we pray for ourselves.
Notes: Direct prayer to God preferred over saintly intercession.

Quote_Text: Let not our religion be to worship the dead. We are to honor them for imitation's sake, but not to worship them for religion's sake.
Notes: Distinction between honor and religious worship.

Quote_Text: We do not build temples unto our martyrs, as unto gods, but we set up memorials for them, as for men departed, whose souls do live in rest with God. Nor do we set up any altars to sacrifice unto them, but we offer our sacrifice unto one only God, both theirs and ours. At which sacrifice they are named in their order as men of God, who have conquered the world by confessing of Him, but they are not invocated of the priest that sacrifices.
Notes: Memorials vs. worship; saints not invocated.

Quote_Text: Those that defended the law drew on the people unto the worship of angels also, saying that the law was given by them. And this vice remained very long in Phrygia and Pisidia, wherefore the synod which met at Laodicea, the metropolitan city of Phrygia, did by a decree prohibit all men from praying unto angels.
Notes: Laodicea condemned angel-prayer — by analogy, saint invocation.

Quote_Text: When we offer this sacrifice, we make mention of them that are departed before us, that God may receive our prayers by their intercession.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Perkins: this is commemoration / petition to God, not direct invocation of saints.

Quote_Text: At the communion, we mention not them as we do the others that rest in peace, or that we should pray for them, but rather that they may pray for us.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Augustine: commemoration at communion is not prayer to saints.

Quote_Text: Although the saints do pray for us, yet shall we be sooner heard when we pray for ourselves.
Notes: Direct prayer surpasses saintly intercession.

Quote_Text: [The souls of the faithful are reserved in secret habitacles out of heaven, not enjoying the sight of God until the last judgment.]
Notes: No immediate beatific vision.

Quote_Text: [Souls of the faithful reserved in secret places out of heaven, not enjoying sight of God until last judgment.]
Notes: Pre-resurrection state.

Quote_Text: [Souls reserved in habitacles out of heaven until last judgment.]
Notes: Origen on intermediate state.

Quote_Text: [Souls reserved in habitacles out of heaven until last judgment.]
Notes: Chrysostom on intermediate state.

Quote_Text: The souls of the dead were kept all in one common receptacle out of heaven until the resurrection.
Notes: Clear statement on intermediate state.

Quote_Text: Every soul is sequestered in the lower places, until the judgment day.
Notes: Intermediate state in lower places.

Quote_Text: Abraham's bosom, that is, the habitation of secret rest, is not to be held any part of hell.
Notes: Augustine denies Limbus Patrum is part of hell.

Quote_Text: There is no middle place for any man to be, but with the devil, that is, not with Christ.
Notes: No middle place between heaven and hell.

Quote_Text: Hell is one place, as I think, and Abraham's bosom another. And I call that region Abraham's bosom which, if it be not heavenly, yet is higher than hell, giving rest unto the souls of the righteous until the consummation of all things terminates their resurrection, with fullness of reward.
Notes: Tertullian's intermediate state theology.

Quote_Text: Christ descended into hell for no other cause but to preach the gospel to the Gentiles, that they might learn the true faith and be saved, or justly be condemned for ever.
Notes: Clement's descent doctrine — considered heretical by some.

Quote_Text: I think it no absurdity to believe that the saints of old that expected Christ in faith were in habitacles far distant from the torments of the wicked, but yet that they were in hell until that Christ's blood and descending thither set them free.
Notes: Augustine on Christ freeing OT saints.

Quote_Text: I think that after the resurrection from the dead, we shall all need a purging, and purifying sacrament, for none can arise again without some stains.
Notes: Origen places purification at resurrection, not between death and judgment.

Quote_Text: After the end of the world, this shall be the future baptism, when iniquity shall be burned away by the furnace, that the just may shine as the sun in the kingdom of God.
Notes: Ambrose places fire at end of world, not between death and judgment.

Quote_Text: All must come to the fire, all must come to this furnace.
Notes: Origen's universal purification — undercuts Roman purgatory.

Quote_Text: Some commit greater venial sins, and some lesser, and therefore it is meet to have them put into the fire of purgatory, now in the space between this and the judgment.
Notes: Bede — one of the clearest pro-purgatory patristic witnesses.

Quote_Text: Because we are redeemed by the grace of our Creator, we have this heavenly gift bestowed upon us, that when we leave our carnal habitation, immediately we are carried unto our heavenly rewards, because seeing that our Maker and Redeemer breaking up the gates of hell, took thence all the souls of the elect, surely He suffers not us to go thither from whence He brought all the rest in His descension.
Notes: Gregory's authentic works undermine his Dialogues on purgatory.

Quote_Text: All the saints are everywhere, and assist all men, and are always with us, and stay still with us, that is, do both keep and defend us while we are in our bodies, and also do receive and entertain us when we leave our bodies.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Perkins: 'boldly affirmed and weakly concluded.' Used ubique for passim.

Quote_Text: Hear also, O you soul of great Constantine, if you have any sense, perceivance, or understanding.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Perkins: rhetorical apostrophe (prosopopoeia), not true invocation.

Quote_Text: Farewell, O Paula, and by your prayers help the decrepit age of him that honors you.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Perkins: apostrophe as if present, not actual invocation.

Quote_Text: Those that behold do embrace, as though the body were living and flourishing, using the eyes, ears, mouth, and instruments of all the senses. And the pouring out the tears of love and duty unto the martyr, as though he were living and present, they offer up their humble prayers unto him, to be an intercessor for them, entreating him as a soldier of God.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Perkins acknowledges Gregory errs here; patristic evidence of error.

Quote_Text: If any man do but stand by these sepulchers, the very sight of the coffin invading his soul penetrates it, and stirs it up, moving therein such an affect that he deems them that lie therein buried, to be visibly present with him, and offering their prayers together with him.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Chrysostom's popular affective language misused to support invocation.

Quote_Text: You do affirm that the souls of the apostles and martyrs are placed either in Abraham's bosom, or the place of repose, or under God's altar, and that they cannot be present at their tombs and where themselves please. If the Lamb be everywhere, then those that are with the Lamb must be believed to be everywhere.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Jerome argues martyrs are everywhere — Perkins says 'boldly affirmed.'

Quote_Text: [Augustine proves by Scripture, namely out of Isaiah 63:16, that the saints know not what is done here below.]
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Saints do not know what occurs on earth.

Quote_Text: [Augustine] leaves it undetermined whether the dead martyrs do help us or no, and adds that these things pass our understanding.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Augustine's agnosticism on saint intercession.

Source_Work: Sermo 1, de Nativitate Petri et Pauli
Quote_Text: Look how much our own sins do cast us down, so much the apostles' merits do raise us up.
Notes: Perkins cites as patristic error — post-400 corruption.

Quote_Text: Obtain that God would pardon us our sin by thy just prayers and merits.
Notes: Cited as evidence fathers erred in invocation.

Quote_Text: [Cyril in his Thesaurus lib. 2, cap. 1:] There is no man that knows not that the Scriptures deny adoration unto every created nature and allow that to God only.
Notes: God alone receives adoration.

Quote_Text: I confidently affirm that whosoever calls himself, or is willing to be called universal priest or bishop, in this loftiness and pride of his, he is the forerunner of Antichrist.
Notes: Gregory condemns the title 'universal bishop.'

Quote_Text: This word of commanding, or command, I pray you take it away, let me hear no more of it; for I know who I am, and who you are. For by your places you are my brethren, in virtues and good examples, my fathers. I have therefore commanded nothing, but I have labored to show what in my opinion is most profitable or convenient.
Notes: Gregory disclaims jurisdictional command.

Quote_Text: [No primacy of power, dominion, jurisdiction, or authority in first 600 years.]
Notes: Chrysostom cited against papal jurisdiction.

Quote_Text: The rest of the apostles were even the very same that Peter was, partakers of the same fellowship with him, both of honor and power.
Notes: Apostles equal to Peter in honor and power.

Quote_Text: The church was built upon all the apostles, and every one of them received the keys of the kingdom of heaven, and the strength of the church was founded on them all equally, yet that among the twelve one was chosen, that a head or chief being appointed, the occasion of schism might be taken away.
Notes: Church built on all apostles, not Peter alone.

Quote_Text: God gave the apostles and their successors full and plenary power in the church.
Notes: Power distributed to all apostles.

Quote_Text: The church was constituted upon bishops, and not upon Peter only.
Notes: Church built on episcopate, not Peter uniquely.

Quote_Text: Peter is called a rock, and has the foundations of the church committed to his trust.
Notes: Perkins uses Ambrose to qualify this as referring to faith, not jurisdiction.

Quote_Text: Peter is called a rock, for that he first laid the foundation of faith in the nations.
Notes: Peter's rock = his foundational preaching of faith, not jurisdiction.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ, not Peter.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [That Peter himself was the rock seemed to Augustine after good deliberation so harsh a speech that he recanted it in his retractions.]
Notes: Augustine retracts his interpretation of Peter as rock.

Quote_Text: Number the priests, even from the very seat of Peter, that is, the rock which the gates of hell cannot overcome.
Notes: Peter's seat = his doctrine / confession, not personal authority.

Quote_Text: [Peter's faith/confession is the rock, not Peter himself.]
Notes: Rock = faith of Peter.

Quote_Text: [Peter's faith/confession is the rock, not Peter himself.]
Notes: Rock = faith of Peter.

Quote_Text: Your faith is the rock.
Notes: Faith = the rock.

Quote_Text: [Peter's faith/confession is the rock, not Peter himself.]
Notes: Rock = Peter's confession.

Quote_Text: [The rock in Matthew 16 is Christ, not Peter.]
Notes: Rock = Christ.

Quote_Text: [The pope is called] Christ by his anointing.
Notes: Cited as example of hyperbolical patristic language about the pope.

Quote_Text: Peter was assumed into the fellowship of the undivided Trinity.
Notes: Extreme hyperbole about Peter — Perkins: not literal doctrine.

Quote_Text: [Basil's soul is in heaven and offers sacrifice, as I suppose (ὡς οἶμαι).]
Notes: Nazianzene hedges with 'as I suppose' — shows he is not asserting doctrine.

Quote_Text: The emperor called that noble Council of Nicaea.
Notes: Councils called by emperors, not popes.

Quote_Text: Peter's chair is the church, where not so much the person of Peter, or his personal successor, as his doctrine is resident.
Notes: Petrine chair = doctrinal teaching, not jurisdictional seat.

Quote_Text: Cyprian was not set over the Church of Carthage only, no, nor only over the churches of Africa, all which have received much honor and renown by him and from him, but he was, as it were, a bishop, even over all the western churches, yea almost over all the eastern also.
Notes: Honorific, not jurisdictional, primacy of Cyprian — parallels Roman claims.

Quote_Text: [Irenaeus believed the bread Christ broke as His body, yet never dreamed of transubstantiation.]
Notes: Real presence without transubstantiation.

Quote_Text: [Justin Martyr held the bread is body, but never dreamed of transubstantiation.]
Notes: Pre-transubstantiation eucharistic theology.

Quote_Text: [Tertullian takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense.]
Notes: Figurative interpretation of institution words.

Quote_Text: [Origen takes 'this is my body' in an allegorical sense.]
Notes: Allegorical/figurative reading.

Quote_Text: [Cyprian takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense.]
Notes: Figurative reading.

Quote_Text: [Nazianzene takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense.]
Notes: Figurative reading.

Quote_Text: [Ambrose takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense in three places.]
Notes: Figurative reading in Ambrose.

Quote_Text: [Jerome takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense in two places.]
Notes: Figurative reading in Jerome.

Source_Work: De Doctrina Christiana; De Trinitate; Epistolae; etc.
Source_Ref: bk. 3, ch. 16; bk. 3, ch. 4 & 10; Ep. 23; In Levit. q. 57; etc.
Quote_Text: [Augustine takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense in seven or eight places.]
Notes: Multiple instances of figurative reading in Augustine.

Quote_Text: [Chrysostom takes 'this is my body' in a figurative sense.]
Notes: Figurative reading.

Quote_Text: Our Lord is above, but here also, and our Lord is truth. For His body wherein He rose again, may be but in one place, but His truth is spread abroad everywhere.
Notes: Christ's body is locally circumscribed — against ubiquity.

Quote_Text: He ascended into heaven, sits at God's right hand, from whence and from no other place He shall come to judgment. In that He is God, He is everywhere; but as He is man, He is but in heaven.
Notes: Christ as man is locally in heaven.

Quote_Text: According to His human substance, he left the earth, when He ascended up to heaven.
Notes: Christ's humanity ascended and departed earth.

Quote_Text: We call that only the body and blood of Christ, which we, taking from the fruits of the earth and consecrating with mystical prayer, do receive in due order, for our soul's health; and as a memorial of our Lord's suffering for us.
Notes: Consecration by prayer, not bare words alone.

Quote_Text: [Augustine makes an opposition between sacramental eating and true (spiritual) eating.]
Notes: True eating = spiritual, not bodily reception.

Quote_Text: Therein the church truly offers herself unto God.
Notes: Church offers herself, not Christ to the Father.

Quote_Text: What is in the cup? That which flowed from His side. And again, that which He suffered not upon the cross, He suffers for you in the offering, suffering Himself to be thrust through.
Notes: Perkins: rhetorical hyperbole, not literal doctrine of repeated sacrifice.

Quote_Text: Will you know what are the heavenly words of consecration? These are they: the priest says, 'Make this oblation ascribed unto us, reasonable and acceptable for us, who the day before His passion took bread in His holy hands...'
Notes: Shows early church used longer narrative, not just 5 words, for consecration.

Quote_Text: The flesh and blood of our Lord is understood two ways: either for His spiritual, and divine flesh and blood, whereof He said, 'My flesh is meat indeed'; or for the flesh which was nailed on the cross, and that blood which was shed by the soldier's spear.
Notes: Jerome's two-nature eucharistic theology.

Quote_Text: Let no man glory in his works, because no man is justified by his deeds. But he that has justice, has it in gifts, because after baptism he is justified. It is therefore faith that frees him through the blood of Christ, because happy is he that has his sins forgiven and his pardon granted.
Notes: Ambrose: justification by faith, not works.

Quote_Text: Woe also be unto the man of most commendable life, if you sift his actions without the favor of mercy.
Notes: No trust in personal merit before God's tribunal.

Quote_Text: Only Christ did undergo punishment for us without any evil desert, that we might attain unto grace by Him, without any good desert.
Notes: Grace without desert.

Quote_Text: What is that? Enter not into judgment with Thy servant. Stand not in judgment with me, nor exact of me all that Thou hast constrained me, and enjoined me, for Thou wilt find me guilty, if Thou enter but in judgment with me. Wherefore I stand in need of Thy mercy, rather than of Thy most easy judgment.
Notes: Augustine's penitential trust in mercy alone.

Quote_Text: If that your good deserts be God's gifts, then He does not crown your good deserts as your own deservings, but as His own gifts.
Notes: Merit is God's gift, not basis of reward.

Quote_Text: The ungodly is justified by faith without any good deserts of his works.
Notes: Justification by faith without works.

Quote_Text: We do gather out of many testimonies, that man is not justified by the precepts of good life, but by the faith of Jesus Christ.
Notes: Classic Augustine sola fide text.

Quote_Text: Eternal rest is prepared for those that have resisted the devil strongly in this life, not as a debt given to their works, but out of the bounteous grace of the most mighty and merciful God.
Notes: Eternal life is not a debt for works.

Quote_Text: If by Himself the judgment of God were sufficient, and if He should deal with us severely according unto our merits, what hope were there remaining for any of us?
Notes: No hope in merits if God judges strictly.

Quote_Text: All my faith is in Christ. By Him only I believe to be justified and saved; He is my rock, He is my refuge.
Notes: Medieval witness to faith in Christ alone.

Quote_Text: He that believes with a strong affection extends his heart wholly unto God, and what then? He is united to him. And his heart being enkindled, conceives a most certain assurance to be made partaker of His desire.
Notes: Assurance through strong faith.

Quote_Text: If you believe that your sins cannot be forgiven but by Him whom only you did sin against, and who Himself cannot sin, you do well. But add this, that you believe that by Him they may be forgiven; this is the testimony that the Holy Ghost sets down in your heart, saying your sins are forgiven you.
Notes: Spirit-wrought certainty of forgiveness.

Quote_Text: Who is righteous but he that recompenses God's love with his love again? And this is never done but when the Holy Ghost by faith reveals unto man God's purpose from everlasting, concerning his future salvation.
Notes: Assurance revealed by the Holy Ghost through faith.

Quote_Text: Here it is most plainly necessary that faith do succor us, that that which lies hidden concerning us in the heart of the Father, may by His Spirit be revealed unto us, and the same Spirit testifying unto us, may persuade our spirits that we are the sons of God.
Notes: Filial assurance by the Spirit.

Source_Ref: pp. 275-276 (PL 158.686-687; historically attributed to Anselm, Admonitio Sancti Anselmi)
Quote_Text: Come then, while life remains in you, in Christ's death alone place your whole trust; in nothing else place any trust; to His death commit yourself wholly; with this alone cover yourself wholly; in this enwrap yourself wholly. And if the Lord your God wishes to judge you, say, 'Lord, between Your judgment and me I present the death of our Lord Jesus Christ; in no other way can I contend with You.' Should God say that you are a sinner, then say, 'O Lord, between my sins and you, I interpose the death of our Lord Jesus Christ.' Should he say that you have merited condemnation, then say, 'O Lord, between my evil merits and you, I place the death of our Lord Jesus Christ. I present his good merits which I ought to have, although I do not have them.' Should he say he is angry toward you, then say, 'O Lord, between your anger and me I place the death of our Lord Jesus Christ.' And when you have said all this, say once again, 'O Lord, I place the death of our Lord Jesus Christ between you and me'
Notes: Alternate translation by Nick Needham (The Doctrine on Which the Church Stands or Falls). Medieval trust in Christ's death alone against divine judgment — imputed merits language.

Quote_Text: Because of the uncertainty of our own justice, and the danger of vain glory, it is most safe and sure to place all our trust upon the only mercy and benignity of God.
Notes: Bellarmine's admission used against Roman confidence in merit.

Quote_Text: The principles of divinity are the very truths of the sacred canon. Moreover, the final resolution of theological discourse appertains unto them, and from them the conclusions of all divinity are drawn.
Notes: Scholastic concession to Scripture's foundational role.

Quote_Text: Not to steal or kill is in every man's power, but to believe that God can do that which is otherwise impossible, this requires a mind elevated, and a more vehement affection towards God, for this is a sign of a worthy and noble love. He honors God that keeps His commandments, but he much more that can argue and dispute through his faith.
Notes: Supererogation = elevated faith and affection, not meritorious excess.

Quote_Text: Are not His precepts so easy that many have gone beyond them by philosophical reason?
Notes: Philosophers exceed external precepts — not moral law.

Quote_Text: The apostle Paul did spend more, because (as himself witnessed) he warred at his own charges.
Notes: Augustine's supererogation = exceeding common practice, not the law.

Quote_Text: The virgins did labor for the beauty of the heart. What do You command us? To abstain from adultery; do You command us this? We do perform more in loving You than You command us, that is, in this commandment.
Notes: Supererogation = exceeding one particular commandment in love.

Quote_Text: We can hardly do one good work without intermixing of some sin or other with it. And it is to be feared, lest when we look for a complete reward of devotion, the punishment for the fault therewith conjoined be not exacted of us.
Notes: Good works contain intermixed sin — supererogation impossible.

Quote_Text: There should no law have been given unto him that has not the due keeping of the law in his own power. And the same freedom of will makes us victors of the same devil.
Notes: Pre-Augustinian free will — Perkins calls this patristic error.

Quote_Text: It is our part to begin, and God's to perfect; we must do what we can, he will fulfill what we cannot.
Notes: Semi-Pelagian free will — patristic error before Augustine's full teaching.

Quote_Text: Unless the patriarch had first done his part, he had not obtained so great things of God. God in His blessings to us does not prevent our wills, but it must begin in ourselves.
Notes: Chrysostom's synergism — patristic error.

Quote_Text: Let us here observe that God in His blessings to us does not prevent our wills, but it must begin in ourselves. And when He sees our minds prompt and ready to receive grace, then He gives us many occasions of salvation.
Notes: Chrysostom's synergism.

Quote_Text: I said that it lives in us to believe and to will, and in God to give the power of working well unto the believing and willing.
Notes: Augustine's earlier (not fully mature) position — he distinguishes.

Quote_Text: Why do we presume so much of the power of nature? It is wounded, maimed, vexed, and lost. Let us confess it freely, and not defend it falsely. Wherefore let us seek the grace of God, not to inform, but to reform it thereby.
Notes: Augustine's mature view of will's bondage.

Quote_Text: Free will has not any power but unto sin, if the way of truth be unknown; and when that which is to be done begins to appear, unless also it delight, and be beloved, there is no doing, no undertaking, no well living.
Notes: Will cannot act toward good without grace.

Quote_Text: Use your marriage with modesty and moderation, and you shall be the chief in the kingdom and enjoy the fullness of blessings.
Notes: Chrysostom affirms married life and kingdom blessing.

Quote_Text: Because we see that divers of the saints, yea, and some apostles, have had wives, we cannot think thus of virginity only.
Notes: Married apostles prove marriage is honored.

Quote_Text: Virginity is equal unto angels; nay, if we seek it willingly, it exceeds them, because in striving in the flesh, it obtains a victory against nature, which the angels have not.
Notes: Cited as patristic hyperbole about virginity.

Quote_Text: O continency, the prophet's joy, the apostle's glory! O continency, the life of angels and the crown of sanctity!
Notes: Hyperbolic praise of virginity.

Quote_Text: Those that are in the flesh, that is, in matrimony, cannot please God.
Notes: Patristic error — denigrating marriage.

Quote_Text: It is evil to contract matrimony.
Notes: Patristic error — Tertullian in Montanist period.

Quote_Text: In the fulfilling the duty of a husband, I fulfilled not the duty of a Christian, and it is an evil thing to touch a woman.
Notes: Jerome's extreme anti-marriage statement.

Quote_Text: The woman's body is polluted by her husband's.
Notes: Patristic error.

Quote_Text: Let those that meddle with the holy mysteries be chaste, and continent, and free from all fleshly uncleanness.
Notes: Celibacy for ministers of sacred rites.

Quote_Text: He that has attained unto true perfection has the apostles for his patterns, and is truly counted a man, not in that he has made a choice of monastical life; but he conquers and excels men, that exercises himself in matrimony and increase of children, and in care and governance of his family, without either pleasure or sorrow.
Notes: Clement holds married life as true perfection.

Quote_Text: [Chrysostom touches Mary with ambition and rashness at the wedding in Cana.]
Notes: Fathers accused Mary of sin — against Immaculate Conception.

Quote_Text: [Origen accuses Mary of infidelity.]
Notes: Fathers accused Mary of sin.

Quote_Text: [Tertullian accuses Mary of infidelity.]
Notes: Fathers accused Mary of sin.

Quote_Text: [Irenaeus calls Mary Eve's advocate hyperbolically — referring to her bringing Christ the Redeemer, not to her intercession.]
Notes: Eve/Mary typology does not support Marian mediation.

Quote_Text: We must beware lest we do not diminish the Son's glory in amplifying of the mother's, and therein provoke the mother to wrath, who had rather have her Son honored than herself, as the Creator ought to be before the creature.
Notes: Even Bonaventure urges restraint in Marian honor.

Quote_Text: I think that he calls the cherubim the thrones (for the prophet Ezekiel saw the throne of the deity placed upon them), and by dominations, principalities, and powers, he means those that governed the nations.
Notes: Theodoret's interpretation overthrows nine-choir schema.

Quote_Text: Whether the archangels be called virtues, or what the difference is between these four words, 'thrones, dominations, principalities, and powers,' let them tell that can, if they prove what they say. I confess plainly, that I am altogether ignorant.
Notes: Augustine's ignorance proves nine choirs is a later innovation.

Quote_Text: [Chrysostom denies that the souls of the dead can return unto the living.]
Notes: Fathers deny souls of dead return to living.

Quote_Text: [Tertullian denies that the souls of the dead can return unto the living.]
Notes: Fathers deny souls of dead return to living.

Quote_Text: [Isidore denies that the souls of the dead can return unto the living.]
Notes: Fathers deny souls of dead return to living.

Quote_Text: [Theophilact denies that the souls of the dead can return unto the living.]
Notes: Fathers deny souls of dead return to living.

Quote_Text: [Augustine seems to be of another opinion — the souls of the dead may appear to the living.]
Notes: Augustine hedges — some tension with other fathers.

Quote_Text: The emperor called a council at Nicaea in Bithynia, setting down a day for all the rulers of every church in all places, to be there present.
Notes: Councils convened by emperors, not popes.

Quote_Text: The king, having a care of the church, called a general council. We bishops, assembled from divers provinces, as Selvica in Isauria, at the command of our high and mighty prince Constantine.
Notes: Emperor commands council, not pope.

Quote_Text: The emperor, without delay called a council of bishops that embraced his faith.
Notes: Emperor convenes councils.

Quote_Text: We went unto his sacred majesty and entreated him to call a council.
Notes: Chrysostom petitions emperor, not pope, for council.

Quote_Text: I cannot find, neither in ancient history, nor in the Acts of the Apostles, that it was a use at any time for the popes alone to call councils.
Notes: Even a future pope admits popes did not call councils.

Quote_Text: Undergo the yoke of Christ... He does not bind your neck with bands, but couples the mind to Him with favor and grace. He does not compel a man by necessity, but directs the willing of good works.
Notes: Fasting is voluntary, not legally compelled.

Quote_Text: Christians, not heretics, but Catholics do abstain both from some sorts of flesh and also from some kinds of fruits, and that for the taming of the flesh... And this use is observed either continually, which few do, or at certain times and days, as almost all do throughout the Lent, everyone as much or as little as he is willing, or as he is able.
Notes: Lent was voluntary, not legally binding.

Quote_Text: What should I stand to relate the often meetings together, and the exercises of the men being placed together in one room, and the women in another apart, and the severe order of discipline, which we as yet do customably observe, and do use to celebrate, especially upon the feast of our Lord's passion, not only in fasting and watchings, but in the diligent hearing of the Holy Scriptures?
Notes: Early fasting was accompanied by Scripture hearing, not mere abstinence.

Quote_Text: If we have done no good by so many admonitions, which I have never omitted to exhort you with in all the time before, and now especially in these seven weeks of fasting, wherein continually, night and day, I have instructed you in the doctrine of the Gospel without all intermission.
Notes: Basil: Lent is for intensive spiritual instruction.

Quote_Text: This fast is not appointed us whereby to render any favor or good turn unto Him that suffered for us, but to confess and acknowledge His passion, which He undertook for us unto our salvation.
Notes: Fasting = acknowledgment of Christ's passion, not meritorious work.

Quote_Text: For this end have we our fastings and our Lent. For this end have we so many holy meetings, and hearing, and hitherto tend all our prayers, and all our doctrine, namely, that being by the diligent meditation of God's precepts cleansed from all the spots of sin, we may receive that unbloody sacrifice.
Notes: Fasting for spiritual cleansing and worthy communion.

Quote_Text: In the primitive church, their fast was alike throughout the whole year, and then devotion beginning to grow cold, and fasting to be neglected, the priests commanded this fast of Lent.
Notes: Lent is ecclesiastical, not apostolic institution.

Quote_Text: [Lent is but merely] a custom risen out of simplicity, and some private institution.
Notes: Lent as private custom, not universal apostolic tradition.

Quote_Text: We ought to fast and abstain, that we do not bind ourselves to the necessity of fasting, lest thereby we should be brought to do a voluntary thing, not of devotion, but of compulsion.
Notes: Fasting must remain voluntary, not compelled.

Quote_Text: Our faith is our defense, and if it be not stricken with distrust when we sign on this fashion, that is, by making this sign, we often do good unto the heathen, God giving us that power.
Notes: Power is from faith and invocation of Christ, not the sign itself.

Quote_Text: He may soon know how much this sign terrifies the devils, that sees how when they are adjured by Christ, they were forced to flee from the bodies they had possessed.
Notes: Power from Christ's name, sign is instrumental.

Quote_Text: This directs our course, together with the faithful calling upon Christ's name, namely, the famous memory of the victorious cross.
Notes: Cross efficacious together with calling on Christ's name.

Quote_Text: Stand stoutly upon this sign, for when the devils see the cross, they remember the crucified.
Notes: Perkins: efficacy comes from remembrance of Christ, not sign ex opere operato.

Quote_Text: Therefore did Luke notify his age, because you should know that he spoke of His manhood. For it is the body, not the deity, that is the object of age. Wherefore, if He increased in the years of man, He also increased in the wisdom of man.
Notes: Christ grew in human wisdom — against omniscience from conception.

Quote_Text: The Lord's Prayer is the breviary of the gospel.
Notes: Lord's Prayer as summary of gospel.

Quote_Text: The law of praying is the law of believing, and the law of working.
Notes: Prayer to God alone establishes faith in God alone.

Quote_Text: [The Platonists] will have no sin unpunished, yet all those punishments, they will have to tend unto reformation, whether they be inflicted by law divine, or human; in this life, or after death.
Notes: Purgatory has pagan philosophical antecedents — Augustine citing Platonists.

Quote_Text: Out of this which is already spoken, we do plainly see that there are some that in that judgment shall be punished with certain purging fires.
Notes: Augustine allows for purging fires at final judgment.

Quote_Text: Being offered for our sins, [Christ] took the name of sin that we might be made the righteousness of God in him, NOT OURS NOR IN US.
Notes: Jerome: righteousness of God in Christ is not ours inherently but imputed.

Quote_Text: The will of the regenerate is kindled only by the Holy Ghost: that they may therefore be able because they will thus: and they will thus, because God works in them to will.
Notes: Regenerate will enabled by Holy Spirit alone.

Quote_Text: We have lost our free will to love God by the greatness of our sin.
Notes: Free will lost through sin.

Quote_Text: Man when he was created, received a great strength in his free will: but by sinning he lost it.
Notes: Free will lost through the fall.

Quote_Text: God gives grace freely to the unworthy, whereby the wicked man being justified is enlightened, with the gift of good will, and with a faculty of doing good: that by mercy preventing him, he may begin to will well, and by mercy coming after he may do the good he will.
Notes: Grace prevents and enables good will.

Quote_Text: It is wholly the grace of God that we are created, healed, saved. To believe and to will is given from above by infusion, and inspiration of the Holy Ghost.
Notes: Bernard: all from grace including faith and will.

Quote_Text: Concupiscence against which the spirit lusteth is sin, because in it there is disobedience against the rule of the mind: and it is the punishment of sin because it befalls man for the merits of his disobedience: and it is the cause of sin.
Notes: Concupiscence is sin, punishment, and cause of sin.

Quote_Text: Charity in some is more, in some less, in some none: the highest degree of all which cannot be increased, is in none, as long as man lives upon earth. And as long as it may be increased, that which is less then it should be, is in fault.
Notes: Concupiscence as moral defect.

Quote_Text: We say that the guilt of concupiscence, not whereby it is guilty (for that is not a person) but that whereby it made man guilty from the beginning, is pardoned, and that the thing itself is evil so as the regenerate desire to be healed of this plague.
Notes: Concupiscence remains as evil after baptism.

Quote_Text: The law of sin in baptism is remitted and not ended.
Notes: Sin's law remitted but not ended at baptism.

Quote_Text: Let not sin reign: he saith not, let not sin be, but let it not reign. For as long as thou livest, of necessity sin will be in thy members: at the least, let it reign not in thee.
Notes: Sin remains after baptism but must not reign.

Quote_Text: The justice of another is assigned unto man: who wanted his own, man was indebted and man made payment. The satisfaction of one is imputed to all. And, why may not justice be from another as well as guiltiness is from another.
Notes: Bernard: alien righteousness imputed to us.

Quote_Text: It suffices me, for all righteousness to have him alone merciful to me, against whom I have sinned. And, not to sin is God's justice, man's justice is the mercifulness of God.
Notes: Man's justice = God's mercy.

Quote_Text: Shall I sing mine own righteousness, Lord I will remember thy righteousness alone: for it is mine also: in that even thou art made unto me righteousness of God. What, shall I fear lest that one be not sufficient for us both? it is not a short cloak that cannot cover two.
Notes: Christ's righteousness is ours — covers both.

Quote_Text: He prays for our faults, and has made our faults his faults, that he might make his justice our justice.
Notes: Exchange of sin and righteousness.

Quote_Text: It is called God's righteousness, because it is not of works, and because it must be without all stain or want: and that cannot be inherent righteousness.
Notes: God's righteousness cannot be inherent.

Quote_Text: He is made sin as we are made justice: not ours but God's, not in us but in him: as he is made sin not his own but ours: not in himself, but in us.
Notes: Anselm: imputed exchange of sin and righteousness.

Quote_Text: [Adam's first sin was the eating of the forbidden fruit which very act is no personal offence, but is imputed to all his posterity.]
Notes: Adam's sin imputed — parallel: Christ's righteousness imputed.

Quote_Text: [Adam's sin is called his handwriting, making us debtors unto God — by imputation.]
Notes: Imputation of Adam's sin as model for imputation of Christ's righteousness.

Quote_Text: [Good works are] the way to the kingdom, not the cause of reigning there.
Notes: Works are means, not causes of salvation.

Quote_Text: The satisfaction made by Christ in his death, and the merit of his death is imputed to every sinner that does believe, for his satisfaction before God.
Notes: Bellarmine's concession — imputed satisfaction.

Quote_Text: Take from heretics the opinions which they maintain with the heathen, that they may defend their questions by Scripture alone, and they cannot stand. Again, We need no curiosity after Christ Jesus, nor inquisition after the Gospel. When we believe it, we desire to believe nothing beside: for this we first believe that there is nothing more which we may believe.
Notes: Tertullian: Scripture alone suffices.

Quote_Text: [Writing of an opinion lacking Scripture authority:] This, because it hath not authority from Scriptures, may as easily be contemned as approved.
Notes: Jerome: lacking Scripture warrant, any opinion may be contemned.

Quote_Text: In those things which are plainly set down in Scripture, are found all those points which contain faith and manners of living well.
Notes: Scripture's plain texts contain all faith and morals.

Quote_Text: The Canon of the Scripture is perfect, and fully sufficient to it self for all things.
Notes: Vincentian canon of Scripture.

Quote_Text: Every form or representation is to be termed an idol.
Notes: Image = idol in religious use.

Quote_Text: The heathen used the names of image and idol indifferently in one and the same signification.
Notes: Image and idol are synonymous in heathen usage.

Quote_Text: Idols are images of dead men.
Notes: Idols = images of dead men.

Quote_Text: Where images are for religions sake, there is no religion.
Notes: Images for religion = no religion.

Quote_Text: We suffer not any to worship Jesus at altars, images, and temples: because it is written, Thou shalt have none other gods.
Notes: Origen forbids worship via images.

Quote_Text: It is against the authority of the Scriptures to see the image of Christ, or of any Saints hanging in the Church. Be mindful beloved children not to bring images into the Church, nor set them in the places where the Saints are buried, but always carry God in your hearts; neither let them be suffered in any common house.
Notes: Epiphanius bans all images from churches.

Quote_Text: [Augustine] in plain terms denies images to be adored.
Notes: Augustine denies adoration of images.

Quote_Text: [Gregory] in plain terms denies images to be adored.
Notes: Gregory denies adoration of images.

Quote_Text: That supplications and thanksgivings are the only perfect sacrifices pleasing God, and that Christians have learned to offer them alone.
Notes: True sacrifice = prayer and thanksgiving, not bodily sacrifice.

Quote_Text: We sacrifice for the health of the Emperor as God hath commanded with pure prayer.
Notes: True sacrifice = pure prayer, not re-offering of Christ.

Quote_Text: This clean offering to be offered in every place, is the prayers of the Saints.
Notes: Clean offering = prayers of saints, not mass.

Source_Work: Liber de Corpore et Sanguine Christi
Quote_Text: Because we sin daily Christ is sacrificed for us mystically, and his Passion is given in mystery. The blood is drunk in mystery spiritually: and it is all spiritual which we eat. The priest distributes to every one not as much as the outward sight giveth, but as much as faith receiveth.
Notes: Paschasius: mystical and spiritual eating only.

Quote_Text: If there be any grace in the water, it is not from the nature of the water, but from the presence of the Spirit.
Notes: Grace in baptism is from the Spirit, not the element.

Quote_Text: Man gives water, but God gives the Holy Ghost.
Notes: God gives grace, not the sacramental act.

Quote_Text: Water touches the body and washes the heart. There is one water of the sacrament; another of the Spirit. The water of the sacrament is visible, the water of the Spirit invisible. That washes the body and signifies what is done in the soul; by this the soul is purged and healed.
Notes: Distinction between outward and inward water.

Quote_Text: Believers are partakers of the body and blood of Christ in baptism.
Notes: Spiritual reception of Christ in baptism.

Quote_Text: It is profitable to be filled with the body of Christ, and drink His blood, not only in mystery, but in knowledge of Holy Scripture.
Notes: Scripture also feeds on Christ's body and blood.

Quote_Text: In baptism we eat and drink the body and blood of Christ.
Notes: Spiritual eating in baptism.

Quote_Text: All Christian men commend each other in their prayers to God. And who prays for all, and for whom none prays, he is that one and true mediator.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Christ alone is universal mediator.

Quote_Text: This saith thy Saviour, thou hast no where to go but to me, thou hast no way to go but by me.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Christ is the only way to God.

Quote_Text: Thou hast no need of patrons to God, or much discourse that thou shouldest sooth others: but though thou be alone and want a patron, and by thyself pray unto God, thou shalt obtain thy desire.
Notes: No patrons needed; pray directly to God.

Quote_Text: Thy prayers have no effect unless they be such as the Lord commends unto thy Father.
Notes: Only Christ-commended prayers reach the Father.

Quote_Text: He being such a man said not, ye have an Advocate, but if any sin we have: he said not ye have, neither said he, ye have me.
Topic: Praying to Mary and the Saints
Notes: Christ is the advocate; saints are not.

Quote_Text: Neither Christ nor His apostles appointed any times of fasting.
Notes: No apostolic command for set fasting times.

Quote_Text: [The early church] fasted of their own accord freely, without law or commandment, as occasions and time served.
Notes: Early fasting was free, not legally prescribed.

Quote_Text: Montanus was the first that made laws of fasting.
Notes: Compulsory fasting originates with the heretic Montanus.

Quote_Text: We must acknowledge the great mercy of God, who puts a difference between sins of ignorance, and such as are done wittingly; and forgives manifold ignorances to them, that know but the foundation and be teachable.
Notes: Melanchthon on implicit faith and God's mercy on ignorance.

Quote_Text: We have no images framed by any base workmen, but such as are brought forth and framed by the word of God, namely patterns of virtue, and frames resembling Christians.
Notes: True images are patterns of virtue in Christians, not material.

Quote_Text: He believes not in God, who putteth not affiance concerning his salvation in God alone.
Notes: True faith = confidence in God alone for salvation.

Quote_Text: [Irenaeus] said that Antichrist should be Lateinus, a Roman.
Notes: Irenaeus identified Antichrist with Rome.

Quote_Text: I think that he as it were passing by the fire shall be saved by the merits and intercessions of the heavenly Church; which does always make intercession for the faithful by request and merit, till Christ shall be complete in his members.
Notes: Lombard on saints' merits — cited and refuted.

Quote_Text: They all therefore were glorified and magnified, not through themselves or their own works or the righteous doing which they wrought, but through His will. And so we, having been called through His will in Christ Jesus, are not justified through ourselves or through our own wisdom or understanding or piety or works which we wrought in holiness of heart, but through faith, whereby the Almighty God justified all men that have been from the beginning; to whom be the glory forever and ever
Notes: Oldest extrabiblical witness to justification by faith; Clement knew the apostles personally.

Quote_Text: Human beings can be saved from the ancient wound of the serpent in no other way than by believing in him who, when he was raised up from the earth on the tree of martyrdom in the likeness of sinful flesh, drew all things to himself and gave life to the dead.
Notes: Salvation only through faith in Christ crucified — no other way of deliverance from sin.

Quote_Text: While I was sick in the flesh, the Savior was sent to me in the likeness of sinful flesh, fulfilling such a dispensation, to redeem me from slavery, from corruption, and from death. And He became to me righteousness, and sanctification, and salvation. Righteousness, by setting me free from sin through faith in Him. Sanctification, in having set me free through water and the Spirit and His word. And salvation, His blood being the ransom of the true Lamb, having given Himself on my behalf.
Notes: Righteousness through faith in Christ, not human works — early patristic sola fide.

Quote_Text: Vainly then do they run about with the pretext that they have demanded Councils for the faith's sake; for divine Scripture is sufficient above all things.
Notes: Divine Scripture sufficient above councils — patristic appeal to Scripture over conciliar authority.

Quote_Text: For concerning the divine and holy mysteries of the Faith, not even a casual statement must be delivered without the Holy Scriptures; nor must we be drawn aside by mere plausibility and artifices of speech. Even to me, who tell you these things, give not absolute credence, unless you receive the proof of the things which I announce from the Divine Scriptures. For this salvation which we believe depends not on ingenious reasoning, but on demonstration of the Holy Scriptures.
Notes: Cyril juxtaposes speech and reason against Holy Scripture — the Word is used to test teaching

Quote_Text: What is the mark of a faithful soul? To be in these dispositions of full acceptance on the authority of the words of Scripture, not venturing to reject anything nor making additions. For, if 'all that is not of faith is sin' as the Apostle says, and 'faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the Word of God,' everything outside Holy Scripture, not being of faith, is sin.
Notes: Everything outside Holy Scripture, not being of faith, is sin — you don't get more Sola Scriptura than that.

Quote_Text: Let the inspired Scriptures then be our umpire, and the vote of truth will be given to those whose dogmas are found to agree with the Divine words.
Notes: Scriptures are used to test dogma — placing the Word over dogma

Source_Work: Homily 8 On Repentance and the Church
Quote_Text: Regarding the things I say, I should supply even the proofs, so I will not seem to rely on my own opinions, but rather, prove them with Scripture, so that the matter will remain certain and steadfast.
Notes: Teachers must prove teaching from Scripture — not private opinion or mere plausibility.

Source_Work: Homily 33 in Acts of the Apostles
Quote_Text: There comes a heathen and says, "I wish to become a Christian, but I know not whom to join: there is much fighting and faction among you, much confusion: which doctrine am I to choose?" How shall we answer him? "Each of you" (says he) "asserts, 'I speak the truth.'" No doubt: this is in our favor. For if we told you to be persuaded by arguments, you might well be perplexed: but if we bid you believe the Scriptures, and these are simple and true, the decision is easy for you. If any agree with the Scriptures, he is the Christian; if any fight against them, he is far from this rule.
Notes: Scripture is the test for believers and unbelievers alike — teachers who do not follow Scripture should not be regarded as Christian.

Source_Work: On the Soul and the Resurrection
Quote_Text: We are not entitled to such license, I mean that of affirming what we please; we make the Holy Scriptures the rule and the measure of every tenet; we necessarily fix our eyes upon that, and approve that alone which may be made to harmonize with the intention of those writings.
Notes: Holy Scripture is the rule and measure of every tenet — no license to affirm what we please apart from it.

Quote_Text: It is impossible either to say or fully to understand anything about God beyond what has been divinely proclaimed to us, whether told or revealed, by the sacred declarations of the Old and New Testaments.
Notes: Knowledge of God bounded by OT/NT revelation — doctrinal limit at Scripture.

Quote_Text: To be righteous does not derive from human achievement, but from a divine gift.
Notes: Righteousness before God is divine gift, not human achievement.

Quote_Text: Grace is before you... you will not be saved by the Law, and will mar this gift also.
Notes: Grace offered without works demanded; insisting on Law-salvation nullifies God's gift.

Quote_Text: He does not say, the wages of your good deeds, but the gift of God... by grace all these things came about.
Notes: Not wages of deeds or return for labor, but gift of God by grace alone.

Source_Work: Homily 8 On Repentance and the Church
Quote_Text: Your wickedness is measurable, but the medicine is immeasurable... God’s philanthropy is unlimited.
Notes: Human wickedness finite; God's philanthropy immeasurable — mercy exceeds our sin.

Quote_Text: What should we resourceless people give Thee in return for all things?... Thou askest nothing of us but that we be saved. Even this Thou hast given us.
Notes: God asks only that we be saved — even that saving grace is his gift.

Quote_Text: Purification from sins is not effected by our merit, but by the compassion of God... even that poor little virtue which we call ours has itself been granted to us by his bounty.
Notes: Purification by God's compassion, not merit; even our virtue is his bounty.

Quote_Text: Confess Jesus Christ, and believe that He is risen from the dead, and you will be saved. For indeed righteousness is only to be believed; but a complete salvation must also be confessed and knowledge must be added to confidence.
Notes: Righteousness is to be believed; salvation requires confession and knowledge added to faith.

Quote_Text: This is perfect and pure boasting in God, when one is not proud on account of his own righteousness but knows that he is indeed unworthy of the true righteousness and is justified solely by faith in Christ
Notes: Basil: justified solely by faith in Christ — not by one's own righteousness.

Quote_Text: But we all escape the condemnation for our sins referred to above, if we believe in the grace of God through His Only-begotten Son, our Lord Jesus Christ, who said: 'This is my blood of the new testament, which shall be shed for many unto the remission of sins.'
Notes: Escape from condemnation through faith in Christ's blood for remission of sins.

Quote_Text: To this end has His Grace and Goodness been formed upon us in Christ Jesus, that being dead according to works, redeemed through faith and saved by grace, we might receive the gift of this great deliverance.
Notes: Redeemed through faith and saved by grace — not by works.

Quote_Text: But when the Lord Jesus came, He forgave all men that sin which none could escape, and blotted out the handwriting against us by the shedding of His own Blood. This then is the Apostle's meaning; sin abounded by the Law, but grace abounded by Jesus; for after that the whole world became guilty, He took away the sin of the whole world, as John bore witness, saying: Behold the Lamb of God, which taketh away the sin of the world. Wherefore let no man glory in works, for by his works no man shall be justified, for he that is just hath a free gift, for he is justified by the Bath. It is faith then which delivers by the blood of Christ, for Blessed is the man to whom sin is remitted, and, pardon granted.
Notes: No man justified by works; faith delivers by the blood of Christ.

Source_Ref: Eight Sermons on Genesis, pp. 123-24 (Robert C. Hill, 2004)
Quote_Text: Let us see, however, whether the brigand gave evidence of effort and upright deeds and a good yield. Far from his being able to claim even this, he made his way into paradise before the apostles with a mere word, on the basis of faith alone, the intention being for you to learn that it was not so much a case of his sound values prevailing as the Lord's lovingkindness being completely responsible. What, in fact, did the brigand say? What did he do? Did he fast? Did he weep? Did he tear his garments? Did he display repentance in good time? Not at all: on the cross itself after his utterance he won salvation. Note the rapidity: from cross to heaven, from condemnation to salvation. What were those wonderful words, then? What great power did they have that they brought him such marvelous good things? "Remember me in your kingdom." What sort of word is that? He asked to receive good things, he showed no concern for them in action; but the one who knew his heart paid attention not to the words but to the attitude of mind.
Notes: Thief on the cross saved by faith alone — not by works or repentance.

Quote_Text: They said that he who adhered to faith alone was cursed; but he, Paul, shows that he who adhered to faith alone is blessed.
Notes: Paul shows that he who adheres to faith alone is blessed — not cursed.

Quote_Text: But he calls it their 'own righteousness,' either because the Law was no longer of force, or because it was one of trouble and toil. But this he calls God's righteousness, that from faith, because it comes entirely from the grace from above, and because men are justified in this case, not by labors, but by the gift of God.
Notes: God's righteousness from faith — not by labors but by the gift of God.

Quote_Text: Here he shows God's power, in that He has not only saved, but has even justified, and led them to boasting, and this too without needing works, but looking for faith only.
Notes: Justified without works — looking for faith only.

Quote_Text: For you believe the faith; why then do you add other things, as if faith were not sufficient to justify? You make yourselves captive, and you subject yourself to the law.
Notes: Faith alone is sufficient to justify — adding works subjects one to the law.

Quote_Text: But what is the 'law of faith?' It is, being saved by grace. Here he shows God's power, in that He has not only saved, but has even justified, and led them to boasting, and this too without needing works, but looking for faith only
Notes: The law of faith is being saved by grace — justified without works.

Quote_Text: We need none of those legal observances, he says; faith suffices to obtain for us the Spirit, and by Him righteousness, and many and great benefits.
Notes: Faith suffices to obtain the Spirit and righteousness — no legal observances needed.

Quote_Text: And he well said, "a righteousness of mine own," not that which I gained by labor and toil, but that which I found from grace. If then he who was so excellent is saved by grace, much more are you. For since it was likely they would say that the righteousness which comes from toil is the greater, he shows that it is dung in comparison with the other. For otherwise I, who was so excellent in it, would not have cast it away, and run to the other. But what is that other? That which is from the faith of God, i.e. it too is given by God. This is the righteousness of God; this is altogether a gift. And the gifts of God far exceed those worthless good deeds, which are due to our own diligence.
Notes: Righteousness from grace — not from labor and toil; gifts of God exceed our own good deeds.

Quote_Text: God does not wait for time to elapse after repentance. You state your sin, you are justified. You repented, you have been shown mercy.
Notes: Immediate justification upon repentance — no waiting period required.

Quote_Text: Suppose someone should be caught in the act of adultery and the foulest crimes and then be thrown into prison. Suppose, next, that judgment was going to be passed against him and that he would be condemned. Suppose that just at that moment a letter should come from the Emperor setting free from any accounting or examination all those detained in prison. If the prisoner should refuse to take advantage of the pardon, remain obstinate and choose to be brought to trial, to give an account, and to undergo punishment, he will not be able thereafter to avail himself of the Emperor's favor. For when he made himself accountable to the court, examination, and sentence, he chose of his own accord to deprive himself of the imperial gift. This is what happened in the case of the Jews. Look how it is. All human nature was taken in the foulest evils. "All have sinned," says Paul. They were locked, as it were, in a prison by the curse of their transgression of the Law. The sentence of the judge was going to be passed against them. A letter from the King came down from heaven. Rather, the King himself came. Without examination, without exacting an account, he set all men free from the chains of their sins. All, then, who run to Christ are saved by his grace and profit from his gift. But those who wish to find justification from the Law will also fall from grace. They will not be able to enjoy the King's loving-kindness because they are striving to gain salvation by their own efforts; they will draw down on themselves the curse of the Law because by the works of the Law no flesh will find justification. What does this mean? That he has justified our race not by right actions, not by toils, not by barter and exchange, but by grace alone. Paul, too, made this clear when he said: "But now the justice of God has been made manifest apart from the Law." But the justice of God comes through faith in Jesus Christ and not through any labor and suffering.
Notes: Justified by grace alone through faith — not by works of the Law.

Quote_Text: What is meant by mercy? and what by sacrifice? By mercy then is signified, Justification and grace in Christ, even that which is by faith. For we have been justified, not by the works of the law that we have done, but by His great mercy. And sacrifice means the law of Moses.
Notes: Justification and grace in Christ by faith — not by works of the law.

Quote_Text: Be not troubled when thou meditatest upon the greatness of thy former sins; but rather know, that still greater is the grace that justifieth the sinner and absolveth the wicked. Faith then in Christ is found to be the pledge to us of these great blessings; for it is the way that leadeth unto life, that bids us go to the mansions that are above, that raises us to the inheritance of the saints, that makes us members of the kingdom of Christ.
Notes: Grace that justifies the sinner exceeds the greatness of former sins; faith is the pledge of these blessings.

Quote_Text: Gain for yourself the pardon coming from faith, since he is his own worst enemy who does not believe that he is given what the very generous Bestower of mercy promises in all kindness.
Notes: Pardon comes from faith — not believing God's promise is self-defeating.

Quote_Text: Give yourself, O man, pardon by believing, since you fell into all the sins by despairing.
Notes: Pardon by believing — despair leads to sin.

Quote_Text: Nevertheless, sacred doctrine makes use of these authorities as extrinsic and probable arguments; but properly uses the authority of the canonical Scriptures as an incontrovertible proof, and the authority of the doctors of the Church as one that may properly be used, yet merely as probable. For our faith rests upon the revelation made to the apostles and prophets who wrote the canonical books, and not on the revelations (if any such there are) made to other doctors. Hence Augustine says (Epis. ad Hieron. xix, 1): "Only those books of Scripture which are called canonical have I learned to hold in such honor as to believe their authors have not erred in any way in writing them. But other authors I so read as not to deem everything in their works to be true, merely on account of their having so thought and written, whatever may have been their holiness and learning."
Notes: Canonical Scripture is incontrovertible proof; Church doctors are merely probable — faith rests on apostolic/prophetic revelation in canonical books (Norma Normans Non Normata).

Quote_Text: Look carefully into the Scriptures, which are the true utterances of the Holy Spirit. Observe that nothing of an unjust or counterfeit character is written in them
Notes: Scriptures as Spirit-inspired utterances containing nothing unjust or counterfeit.

Quote_Text: Take up the epistle of the blessed Apostle Paul. What did he write to you at the time when the Gospel first began to be preached? Truly, under the inspiration of the Spirit, he wrote to you concerning himself, and Cephas, and Apollos, because even then parties had been formed among you
Notes: Paul's Corinthian epistle cited as Spirit-inspired apostolic writing when the Gospel was first preached.

Quote_Text: Mark, having become the interpreter of Peter, wrote down accurately, though not indeed in order, whatsoever he remembered of the things done or said by Christ. For he neither heard the Lord nor followed him, but afterward, as I said, he followed Peter, who adapted his teaching to the needs of his hearers, but with no intention of giving a connected account of the Lord's discourses, so that Mark committed no error while he thus wrote some things as he remembered them. For he was careful of one thing, not to omit any of the things which he had heard, and not to state any of them falsely. These things are related by Papias concerning Mark. But concerning Matthew he writes as follows: 'So then Matthew wrote the oracles in the Hebrew language, and every one interpreted them as he was able.' And the same writer uses testimonies from the first Epistle of John and from that of Peter likewise. And he relates another story of a woman, who was accused of many sins before the Lord, which is contained in the Gospel according to the Hebrews. These things we have thought it necessary to observe in addition to what has already been stated
Notes: Papias on Mark and Matthew; cites Johannine and Petrine epistles; mentions Gospel according to the Hebrews (preserved in Eusebius).

Quote_Text: Since I am entirely convinced that no Scripture contradicts another, I shall admit that I do not understand what is recorded, and shall strive to persuade those who imagine that the Scriptures are contradictory, to be rather of the same opinion of myself
Notes: Scriptures are harmoniously non-contradictory; apparent difficulties admit ignorance rather than contradiction.

Quote_Text: How much more, then, shall we know the truth who are instructed by the holy prophets, who were possessed by the Holy Spirit of God! On this account all the prophets spoke harmoniously and in agreement with one another, and foretold the things that would come to pass in all the world
Notes: Prophets possessed by the Spirit spoke harmoniously and in agreement with one another.

Quote_Text: The Scriptures are indeed perfect, since they were spoken by the Word of God and His Spirit
Notes: Scriptures are perfect as spoken by the Word of God and His Spirit.

Quote_Text: All Scripture, which has been given to us by God, is perfectly consistent. The parables harmonize with the passages that are plain; and statements with a clearer meaning serve to explain the parables
Notes: All God-given Scripture is perfectly consistent; plain and parabolic passages harmonize.

Quote_Text: The apostles, likewise, being disciples of the truth, are above all falsehood; for a lie has no fellowship with the truth, just as darkness has none with light, but the presence of the one shuts out that of the other. Our Lord, therefore, being the truth, did not speak lies
Notes: Apostles as disciples of truth cannot speak lies; Christ the truth did not lie.

Quote_Text: But since the writings of Moses are the words of Christ . . . He thus indicates in the clearest manner that the writings of Moses are His words. If, then, [this be the case with regard] to Moses, so also, beyond a doubt, the words of the other prophets are His [words]
Notes: Moses and the prophets speak the words of Christ.

Quote_Text: The statements of Holy Scripture will never be discordant with truth
Notes: Holy Scripture is never discordant with truth.

Quote_Text: To the man who is a peacemaker in either sense there is in the Divine oracles nothing crooked or perverse, for they are all plain to those who understand. And because to such an one there is nothing crooked or perverse, he sees therefore abundance of peace in all the Scriptures, even in those which seem to be at conflict, and in contradiction with one another. And likewise he becomes a third peacemaker as he demonstrates that that which appears to others to be a conflict in the Scriptures is no conflict, and exhibits their concord and peace, whether of the Old Scriptures with the New, or of the Law with the Prophets, or of the Gospels with the Apostolic Scriptures, or of the Apostolic Scriptures with each other. . . . For he knows that all the Scripture is the one perfect and harmonised instrument of God, which from different sounds gives forth one saving voice to those willing to learn
Notes: All Scripture is one perfect harmonised instrument of God; apparent conflicts are reconciled.

Quote_Text: We shall reasonably be thought to have furnished a scandal to the heretics, not assuredly by the fault of the heavenly Scriptures, which never deceive; but by the presumption of human error, whereby they have chosen to be heretics
Notes: Heavenly Scriptures never deceive; heresy arises from human error, not Scripture.

Quote_Text: But they were written by unlearned and ignorant men, and should not therefore be readily believed. See that this be not rather a stronger reason for believing that they have not been adulterated by any false statements, but were put forth by men of simple mind, who knew not how to trick out their tales with meretricious ornaments
Notes: Scriptures written by simple men without rhetorical artifice — not adulterated with false statements.

Quote_Text: For there is no contradiction nor absurdity in the Holy Scriptures
Notes: Holy Scriptures contain no contradiction or absurdity.

Quote_Text: It is the opinion of some that the Scriptures do not agree or that the God who gave them is false. But there is no disagreement at all. Far from it! The Father, who is truth, cannot lie
Notes: Scriptures agree; God who gave them is truth and cannot lie.

Quote_Text: We however, who extend the accuracy of the Spirit to the merest stroke and tittle, will never admit the impious assertion that even the smallest matters were dealt with haphazard by those who have recorded them
Notes: Spirit-inspired accuracy extends to the smallest details of Scripture.

Source_Ref: Books 2 and 3, Chapter 69 Against the Arians, Section 55.7 (Brill Edition, p. 382)
Quote_Text: And thus everything is crystal clear, and nothing in the sacred scripture is contradictory or has any taint of death
Notes: Sacred Scripture is clear and free from contradiction.
